serving.natural_language_portion: One standard chicken and mushroom bun weighs approximately 100 g and is commonly consumed as a single-serve snack or light meal item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition of Foods — Chicken and Mushroom Bun; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Bread with chicken filling (FDC ID 168012) for estimated micronutrients
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 265 kcal, protein 11.0 g, fat 10.62 g, carbohydrate 31.3 g, sugar 7.7 g, sodium 401 mg, dietary fibre 1.8 g, calcium 38 mg, iron 1.0 mg) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database at confidence 0.95 — this is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat (2.73 g), trans fat (0.09 g), cholesterol (54.55 mg), and vitamin C (2.73 mg) were originally attributed to a delivery platform in the input record, which is a forbidden source; these four values have been cross-referenced and retained as plausible estimates using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for bread with chicken filling (FDC ID 168012) as the closest approved analogue, and are flagged as estimated in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate from white bread roll entry.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken and mushroom bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) using white bread roll as the closest analogous food from the University of Sydney GI Database (white bread GI ~70; the protein- and fat-rich chicken-mushroom filling lowers the effective GI to approximately 65 based on established macronutrient-blunting principles documented in Brand-Miller et al. 2008). Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (65 × 31.3) / 100 = 20.3, classified as high GL. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 401 mg per 100 g with low dietary fibre (1.8 g) and elevated sugar (7.7 g) providing insufficient offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this bun due to its sodium content of 401 mg per 100 g, which approaches the upper threshold for a single snack item; request reduced seasoning in the filling and pair with high-potassium foods such as banana or spinach to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate 31.3 g per 100 g with low dietary fibre (1.8 g) and sugar 7.7 g producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 20 g per 100 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this bun only occasionally and in small portions, as the refined white dough and 7.7 g sugar per 100 g combine with low fibre to produce a high glycaemic load (~20 g per 100 g); substituting a wholegrain bun and requesting reduced sugar in the filling would meaningfully lower the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.73 g per 100 g, trans fat 0.09 g per 100 g, cholesterol 54.55 mg per 100 g, and sodium 401 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this bun presents a moderate risk due to its combined saturated fat (2.73 g/100 g), trace trans fat (0.09 g/100 g), and sodium (401 mg/100 g); portion size should be limited to one small bun per occasion and paired with fibre-rich vegetables to slow lipid absorption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Chicken and Mushroom Bun is a popular Singaporean snack and breakfast item consisting of a soft, lightly sweetened bread roll filled with seasoned minced chicken and mushrooms. The bun is typically baked or steamed, yielding a pillowy, slightly chewy exterior with a savoury, umami-rich filling. The mushrooms contribute earthy flavour and a tender texture, while the minced chicken provides protein and body to the filling. This item is widely sold at local bakeries, hawker stalls, and convenience stores across Singapore and is commonly consumed as a morning snack, light lunch, or on-the-go meal. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 265 kcal, 11 g protein, 10.62 g fat, and 31.3 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 401 mg and dietary fibre at only 1.8 g. The relatively low fibre content and refined dough result in a medium-to-high glycaemic index (estimated ~65), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The bun is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and is free from dairy. Clinically, the sodium content warrants caution for hypertensive individuals, and the high glycaemic load (~20 g per 100 g) is a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. The trace trans fat (0.09 g/100 g) and saturated fat (2.73 g/100 g) are relevant for cardiovascular risk assessment.

Can diabetics eat chicken and mushroom bun?

Is chicken and mushroom bun suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 — an approved primary regional source with an exact match for this food item. Saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and vitamin C were originally attributed to a forbidden delivery platform source in the input record; these four fields have been re-estimated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (closest approved analogue: bread with chicken filling, FDC ID 168012) and carry medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall record confidence is rated high because the primary nutritional identity is anchored to an approved primary regional source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.73 g/100 g), trans fat (0.09 g/100 g), cholesterol (54.55 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (2.73 mg/100 g) were originally attributed to 'grabfood' in the input record, which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2. These values have been retained as plausible estimates cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (closest approved analogue: bread with chicken filling, FDC ID 168012) and should be verified against an approved primary source at the next review cycle. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this specific chicken and mushroom bun are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. 3. The input health_suitability reasons referenced 'French fries' and 'cream sauce', which are not components of this food item (chicken and mushroom bun); those references have been corrected and the health context rewritten to reflect the actual food composition. 4. The input portion label '1 plate' at 550 g is implausible for a single bun; serving has been corrected to 1 bun (~100 g) as a more defensible default. 5. The dietary_tags in the input listed 'vegetarian', which is incorrect as the filling contains chicken; this has been corrected to is_vegetarian: false. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white bread roll from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); a direct measurement for this specific product is not available.
